display_name: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
role_summary: Emmett W. Bassett (1921-2013): the first African American to earn a doctoral degree in dairy technology (Ohio State, 1955); one of the last students trained by George Washington Carver at Tuskegee Institute; professor of microbiology at Columbia University Medical School and the New Jersey College of Medicine and Dentistry (retired 1987); civil rights activist from boyhood. Priscilla Tietjen Bassett: his wife and civil rights co-organizer; they met at a 1955 Massachusetts protest of a segregated restaurant raising money for Emmett Till's mother Mamie Till-Mobley; parents of Mary T. Bassett, former New York City Health Commissioner.
ai_reading: The Bassetts' top semantic neighbor is Robert L. Carter (entry 108) at a cosine count of 159; the broader cluster includes Jennifer Lawson (entry 59), Charles McLaurin (entry 17), and Betty Garman Robinson (entry 7). The Carter pairing is the catalog's clearest example of a Northern-professional-couple-and-NAACP-LDF cosine: the Bassetts' multi-decade Columbia and New Jersey medical-faculty activism in the New York / North-Eastern civil rights organizing tradition rhymes vector-wise with Carter's NAACP General Counsel work, both reading as institutionalized-Northern movement professionals rather than as field organizers. The novel observation this joint page surfaces is the meet-at-a-Till-fundraising-protest signature: the Bassetts' marriage began at a 1955 Massachusetts protest raising money for Mamie Till-Mobley's funeral expenses for her son Emmett (catalog external_figure page); the Bassetts' embedding signature reads continuous with the broader civil-rights-movement-recruitment-via-Till-imagery the catalog documents on the Till page and the Parker/Wright eyewitness pages.
biographical_paragraph: While the Bassetts' LoC oral history carries a publication-block audit tier (known issues prevent full publication-grade certification, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external sources before citation), their external biographical record is documented in regional press [src: 1, src: 2]. Emmett W. Bassett was born in 1921 in Henry County, Virginia. He served in World War II, then attended Tuskegee Institute where he assisted George Washington Carver with research, becoming one of the last students Carver trained before his 1943 death. He enrolled at the University of Massachusetts Amherst in 1950 and earned his doctorate in dairy technology from Ohio State University in 1955, the first African American to do so. He and Priscilla Tietjen met at a Massachusetts protest of a segregated restaurant that was raising money for Mamie Till-Mobley's funeral expenses for her son Emmett Till (the August 1955 lynching anchor event documented in this catalog's emmett-till external_figure entry) [src: 2]. They married across racial lines at a moment when such marriages were still illegal in many states (Loving v. Virginia would not be decided until 1967). They moved to New York and joined multiple civil rights organizations in the city, attending the 1963 March on Washington together. Emmett Bassett joined the Columbia University Medical School faculty and subsequently the New Jersey College of Medicine and Dentistry (later UMDNJ), where he served as associate professor of microbiology until his 1987 retirement. He died September 29, 2013 in Grahamsville, New York at ninety-two [src: 2]. Their daughter Mary T. Bassett later served as New York City Health Commissioner under Mayor Bill de Blasio (2014-2018).

interview_snippets.speaker: Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked how she first came to progressive politics, Priscilla Tietjen Bassett describes the New Jersey grade school that shaped her:
interview_snippets.quote: Well, I went to an elementary school in Plainfield, New Jersey, which was as we look back now during the progressive era, Harold Rugg textbooks and just a generally New Deal-enlightened environment.

interview_snippets.speaker: Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
interview_snippets.lead_in: Priscilla recalls the segregated roadhouse on the road between Smith College and Amherst where the picket line formed, the protest at which she and Emmett met:
interview_snippets.quote: And this roadhouse became rather notorious because they refused to serve blacks. So some of the Smith women went over to join a picket line. And whether this is apocryphal or whether that is really the first time we met, that's what I remember.

interview_snippets.speaker: Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
interview_snippets.lead_in: Reflecting on her union organizing at a General Motors plant in Columbus, Priscilla describes how those experiences set the course of her activism:
interview_snippets.quote: They confirmed it, and they also gave me the sense that I could do something about it. And that's really been the guiding light that you don't just sit back and let it happen.

interview_snippets.speaker: Emmett W. Bassett
interview_snippets.lead_in: Emmett W. Bassett explains why he turned down a postwar teaching post that would have trained returning Black veterans to farm on white-owned plantations:
interview_snippets.quote: So I just told them one day that I could never be a person that's training somebody to be a sharecropper.

interview_snippets.speaker: Emmett W. Bassett
interview_snippets.lead_in: Emmett recounts the Columbus fundraising effort he and Priscilla organized for the mother of Emmett Till after the 1955 lynching:
interview_snippets.quote: We had a big meeting at one of the Baptist Church. I think we raised $3,000.

interview_snippets.speaker: Emmett W. Bassett
interview_snippets.lead_in: After finishing his doctorate, Emmett flew to Chicago to interview for dairy-industry jobs and was repeatedly told the firms could not hire a Black scientist:
interview_snippets.quote: And they all said, if they hired me, the whole top would go off of that building. That's what they said.

movement_context: The 1955 Massachusetts protest where the Bassetts met sat inside Mamie Till-Bradley's October-November 1955 NAACP fundraising tour, a thirty-three-city, nineteen-state circuit organized in the weeks after the September 23, 1955 acquittal of Roy Bryant and J. W. Milam in Sumner, Mississippi; by one contemporary NAACP estimate Till-Bradley spoke to more than one hundred thousand people on that tour, and the local-protest-and-fundraising infrastructure that supported it became a recruitment substrate for the next decade of Northeast civil rights organizing [src: 3]. The Bassetts' marriage was contracted nine years before the June 24, 1964 founding of the Medical Committee for Human Rights (MCHR) at Dr. Tom Levin's New York City home, when roughly one hundred Northern physicians, nurses, psychologists, and social workers formalized the Freedom Summer medical-support organization the next month [src: 4, 5]. MCHR's four-point Freedom Summer program (first-aid for civil rights workers and local Black residents, hospital-access support, coordination with existing Black and white medical staff in the state, and desegregation of southern public-health clinics) ran through the summer of 1964; the September 1964 national formalization spawned local affiliates in both the North and the South, with the New York City affiliate working in the same Manhattan and outer-borough networks the Bassetts had joined a decade earlier [src: 4, 5]. MCHR returned to Alabama in March 1965 for the Selma-to-Montgomery march, with almost one hundred physicians and nurses providing medical care along the March 21-25 march route and setting up emergency clinics in Black churches along the highway; the Selma deployment was the organization's largest single-event medical mobilization [src: 4]. The post-1964 medical-support infrastructure MCHR seeded included the Tufts-Delta Health Center at Mound Bayou, Mississippi, co-founded in 1965 by H. Jack Geiger (entry 54 in this catalog) and Tufts colleague Count Gibson with an Office of Economic Opportunity grant; the Delta Health Center became the first rural community health center in the United States and the institutional template for the federally-qualified community-health-center model [src: 6]. The interracial-marriage civil rights organizing the Bassetts participated in across the Northeast and Mid-Atlantic during the 1950s and 1960s ran on a legal substrate that remained criminalized in much of the South: at the time of the Bassetts' marriage, twenty-four states had anti-miscegenation statutes on the books, and the federal ruling that struck them down, Loving v. Virginia, 388 U.S. 1, did not come down until June 12, 1967, twelve years into their marriage [src: 7].
legacy_and_reception: Emmett Bassett's 1987 retirement from the New Jersey College of Medicine and Dentistry (now Rutgers New Jersey Medical School) closed a thirty-two-year academic career that began with his 1955 Ohio State dairy-technology doctorate and ran through the Columbia University Medical School faculty appointment and the subsequent associate-professor-of-microbiology tenure at the New Jersey institution [src: 2]. His and Priscilla's oral history was recorded by Joseph Mosnier on July 21, 2011 at their Grahamsville, New York home as entry 38 of the Civil Rights History Project, the joint Library of Congress and National Museum of African American History and Culture (NMAAHC) initiative authorized by the 2009 Civil Rights History Project Act; the LoC American Folklife Center catalogues it as AFC 2010/039 and the NMAAHC parallel record sits at object identifier nmaahc_2011.174.38.1a-j [src: 1, src: 8]. Emmett Bassett died September 29, 2013 in Grahamsville at ninety-two; the local newspaper of record for the Catskill region, The River Reporter, ran the community obituary that became the canonical short-form biographical source [src: 2]. The Mary T. Bassett line of the family record continues the public-health-and-civil-rights through-line: their daughter served as New York City Health Commissioner from 2014 to 2018 under Mayor Bill de Blasio and later as New York State Commissioner of Health from December 2021 through August 2023, an institutional extension of the Bassett family's mid-century medical-faculty-and-civil-rights record into the present-day public-health-administration record.

gallery.alt: SNCC broadside recruiting volunteers for the 1964 Mississippi Freedom Summer Project
gallery.caption: Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC) recruitment broadside from spring 1964 calling for one thousand student volunteers to staff the Mississippi Freedom Summer Project, the COFO-led voter-registration drive that the Medical Committee for Human Rights (MCHR) was founded in June 1964 to provide medical support for. The Bassetts were married in the Northeast civil rights organizing networks the MCHR's New York affiliate drew on, nine years before the Freedom Summer recruitment effort this broadside represents.
gallery.photographer: Mississippi Department of Archives and History (institutional scan; original broadside printer unknown)
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Flickr Commons)
gallery.license: No known copyright restrictions (Flickr Commons designation)

gallery.alt: Marchers resting on a wall during the Selma to Montgomery civil rights march, March 1965; a sign reads 'We march together, Catholics, Jews, Protestant, for dignity and brotherhood of all men under God, Now!'
gallery.caption: Peter Pettus photograph of marchers resting on a wall during the Selma-to-Montgomery voting-rights march of March 21-25, 1965. The sign reads: 'We march together, Catholics, Jews, Protestant, for dignity and brotherhood of all men under God, Now!' The Medical Committee for Human Rights (MCHR), founded in June 1964 to provide medical support for Mississippi Freedom Summer, deployed almost one hundred physicians and nurses to Selma in March 1965 to provide first-aid and emergency-clinic services along the march route, the organization's largest single-event medical mobilization. Library of Congress Prints and Photographs Division, digital ID cph.3d02329.
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Library of Congress Prints and Photographs Division)
gallery.license: Public domain (U.S. publication 1931-1977 without copyright notice)

gallery.alt: Mildred and Richard Loving, plaintiffs in Loving v. Virginia, photographed by United Press International on June 12, 1967
gallery.caption: United Press International photograph of Mildred and Richard Loving from June 12, 1967, the day the U.S. Supreme Court issued the unanimous Loving v. Virginia opinion (388 U.S. 1) striking down state anti-miscegenation statutes nationwide. The Bassetts' interracial marriage predated the Loving ruling by approximately twelve years; the Northeast civil rights organizing networks they participated in across the 1950s and 1960s operated in a legal landscape in which twenty-four states still criminalized interracial marriage at the time they married.
gallery.license: Public domain (U.S. publication 1931-1977 without copyright notice)

sources.title: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Grahamsville, New York, 2011 July 21

sources.title: Emmett W. Bassett (community obituary)
sources.publisher: The River Reporter, Catskill region newspaper of record, October 2013 (canonical short-form biographical source documenting the September 29, 2013 death at ninety-two and the Tuskegee, Ohio State, Columbia Medical School, and New Jersey College of Medicine and Dentistry career arc)

sources.title: Ruth Feldstein, Grief Capital, Grief Activism: The Brief Life of Mamie Till Bradley's NAACP Tour
sources.publisher: Princeton University scholarly record (the Princeton-archived scholarly study of the October-November 1955 NAACP-organized Mamie Till-Bradley fundraising tour that crossed thirty-three cities in nineteen states; the Northeast leg of the tour generated the protest-and-fundraising local infrastructure in which the Bassetts met)

sources.title: John Dittmer, The Good Doctors: The Medical Committee for Human Rights and the Struggle for Social Justice in Health Care
sources.publisher: Bloomsbury Press, 2009 (paperback reprint University Press of Mississippi, 2017); the standard institutional history of the Medical Committee for Human Rights from its June 1964 founding in Dr. Tom Levin's New York apartment through its Freedom Summer medical-support program, its March 1965 Selma deployment, and its post-1968 Chicago Democratic Convention work

sources.title: Medical Committee for Human Rights (institutional entry)
sources.publisher: SNCC Digital Gateway, Duke University Libraries (the SNCC-organized digital-archive entry on MCHR, documenting the June 24, 1964 founding meeting at Dr. Tom Levin's New York home, the Mississippi Freedom Summer medical-support deployment, the four-point service program, the September 1964 national formalization, and the local-affiliate structure)

sources.title: Delta Health Center, Our History
sources.publisher: Delta Health Center, Inc., Mound Bayou, Mississippi (institutional history of the 1965 founding by Tufts physicians H. Jack Geiger and Count Gibson with Office of Economic Opportunity funding; documents the Delta Health Center's standing as the first rural community health center and the first federally qualified community health center in the United States)

sources.title: Loving v. Virginia: 'Banished' for Love
sources.publisher: Library of Congress Law Library, In Custodia Legis blog (LoC institutional commentary on Loving v. Virginia, 388 U.S. 1 (1967), the unanimous June 12, 1967 Warren Court opinion striking down state anti-miscegenation statutes nationwide; the federal ruling came twelve years into the Bassetts' marriage and ended the criminal-statute landscape in which twenty-four states had still prohibited interracial marriage at the time they were married)

sources.title: Emmett W. Bassett, Ph.D. and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett Oral History Interview
sources.publisher: National Museum of African American History and Culture, Smithsonian Institution (NMAAHC parallel record for the joint LoC-NMAAHC Civil Rights History Project interview; object identifier nmaahc_2011.174.38.1a-j, the institutional record of the partnership that produced the 2011 oral history)
